title: Convention

jokes.text: Roger Furlong quotes Scripture — 'Wherefore by their fruits ye shall know them' — as self-aggrandizing introduction at the convention
jokes.setup: Solemn biblical framing: 'I always refer them to the same passage of the Good Book. The Scriptures.'
jokes.punchline: The verse he chose is about judging people by their deeds — Furlong is a reprehensible person quoting a verse about moral accountability to describe himself positively
jokes.explanation: The irony is rich — Furlong, a monstrous figure, uses a verse about moral character to introduce himself. Works well for fans who know the character, but subtle enough to miss on first viewing. Good economy and character specificity.

jokes.text: Selina coaching Catherine on how to say 'I love you, Mom' like she means it, then immediately saying it in an even more forced way
jokes.setup: Catherine rehearses 'I love you, Mom' robotically; Selina corrects her
jokes.punchline: Selina's demonstration 'I love you, Mom. I love you.' is equally hollow and performative — the politician cannot model genuine emotion
jokes.explanation: Perfectly integrates character — Selina is so consumed by performance and optics that she literally cannot model authentic love for her own daughter. The joke is entirely earned through character consistency. The structure is a clean reversal: teacher performs worse than student.

jokes.text: Jason asserting he's clearly a man because he hasn't been carded 'for years. Like, three years.'
jokes.setup: Debate over whether Catherine should call Jason a 'boy' or a 'man' for the speech
jokes.punchline: Jason's defense of his manhood is that he hasn't been carded for three years — a pathetically low bar
jokes.explanation: Quick, well-structured joke. The 'three years' specificity is the key — it reveals his youth while he tries to assert adulthood. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Mike describing Karen: 'I cannot take Karen anymore. It's been weeks and she has yet to speak a declarative sentence.' / 'Like living next to a highway.'
jokes.setup: Karen has been on the team for weeks giving non-answers
jokes.punchline: The highway analogy — you don't listen, you just tune it out — perfectly captures Karen's useless verbal noise
jokes.explanation: Crisp observation with a memorable image. 'Has yet to speak a declarative sentence' is a sharp, precise complaint. The highway analogy lands because it perfectly describes reflexive tuning-out.

jokes.text: 'It sounds like you've been kidnapped by the Taliban.'
jokes.setup: Catherine's speech delivery sounds angry and forced
jokes.punchline: Selina's correction is that it sounds like a hostage situation rather than a loving daughter's endorsement
jokes.explanation: Sharp, specific, and darkly funny. The image of a hostage tape is vivid and exact. Economy is near-perfect — one sentence does maximum work. Slightly edgy but in the Veep register.

jokes.text: Karen's response 'Exactly' to the Taliban kidnapping line — as if it were a reasonable, measured point
jokes.setup: Selina makes an outrageous comparison about Catherine's delivery
jokes.punchline: Karen, who never takes a position, responds 'Exactly' — her first seemingly declarative statement is in agreement with something absurd
jokes.explanation: Single-word joke with perfect character integration. Karen never commits to anything, so her 'Exactly' agreeing with the Taliban comparison is both consistent with her character (she parrots others) and a perfect punchline. The economy is flawless.

jokes.text: Zucchini farmer: 'If you eat a zucchini anywhere in mainland North America, there's a 20% chance it's one of my zucchini.'
jokes.setup: Dan is being introduced to a potential client — a zucchini magnate
jokes.punchline: The man's pride in his zucchini market share delivered with total seriousness, as if this is major-league power
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '20%' is the joke — this man has actually calculated his zucchini market share and is proud. The absurdity of treating vegetable dominance as political leverage works in the DC context.

jokes.text: Dan: 'I practically photosynthesize.'
jokes.setup: Dan is schmoozing the zucchini farmer, claiming to be a zucchini fan
jokes.punchline: The absurd overclaim — he doesn't just eat zucchini, he photosynthesizes like a plant — is vintage Dan Egan sycophancy taken to logical extreme
jokes.explanation: Classic Dan — shameless, instant, and perfectly calibrated sycophancy. 'Photosynthesize' is an unexpected and precise word choice. The economy is excellent: one word does all the work of establishing Dan's bottomless willingness to say anything.

jokes.text: 'A Rolodex as crammed as a drug mule's poop pocket'
jokes.setup: Praising Dan's Washington connections to the zucchini client
jokes.punchline: The drug mule simile is shockingly crude and specific for a business introduction
jokes.explanation: Inventive and disgusting in equal measure. 'Poop pocket' is memorably crude while 'crammed' does double work (full Rolodex / full drug mule). High originality for the simile itself.

jokes.text: 'Probably not fucking zucchini, right?' — the zucchini farmer laughing at his own joke about not eating zucchini
jokes.setup: The farmer built his entire identity around zucchini dominance
jokes.punchline: He suggests they eat something other than zucchini — he's tired of his own product despite his evangelical pitch
jokes.explanation: A quick callback that deflates his own pomposity. The profanity lands because it's so casual after the grandiose zucchini pride speech.

jokes.text: Dan's only contact is Jonah Ryan: 'You ever hear of Jonah Ryan? No. Old buddy of mine. We go way back. Love that guy.'
jokes.setup: Dan is supposed to be a well-connected White House insider with a contact-stuffed Rolodex
jokes.punchline: The only person he can get in a room is Jonah — the show's established buffoon — and Dan performs fake enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy — Dan's spectacular fall from grace is embodied in his having to call in Jonah as his 'big dog.' The fake warmth ('love that guy') while saying a name that means nothing to the client is a nice structural beat.

jokes.text: Kiss rehearsal: 'Which part?' / 'All of it.' — Catherine and Jason's on-stage kiss is terrible and the whole team watches in horror
jokes.setup: Catherine and Jason are rehearsing their public introduction; Selina commands 'now a kiss'
jokes.punchline: The kiss is so bad that even when asked which part needs work, the answer is 'all of it' — implying they cannot perform basic human affection on cue
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe beat. The visual of a terrible rehearsal kiss judged by political staffers is inherently funny. 'All of it' is the perfect economy response.

jokes.text: 'All sciences are precise. That's what science means.' / 'That's open to interpretation.'
jokes.setup: Selina says 'human emotion... it's not a precise science'; Amy corrects her factually
jokes.punchline: Karen responds 'That's open to interpretation' to Amy's factual, definitional statement about science — her compulsive both-sidesing applied to a tautology
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke. Karen's character flaw — inability to commit to any position — is applied to a statement that is definitionally unambiguous. The absurdity of finding gray area in 'science is precise' is sharp and deeply character-specific. High quotability as a standalone comment on political equivocation.

jokes.text: 'Ugh, sounds good' — Selina's immediate positive reaction to hearing there's a death row execution problem
jokes.setup: Sue announces 'there's a death row inmate in Louisiana'
jokes.punchline: Selina's reflexive 'sounds good' — as if an execution crisis is welcome news — before she knows the details
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — two words that expose Selina's entire character. She hears 'death row' and thinks 'controversy I can spin' before processing that it might be a problem. Deeply character-integrated.

jokes.text: 'He's been half-acuted' / 'Like being decapitated but surviving. Happens to chickens a lot.'
jokes.setup: The execution drug cocktail didn't work — the inmate is 'half dead'
jokes.punchline: Mike's invented word 'half-acuted' and Ben's matter-of-fact comparison to chicken decapitation
jokes.explanation: 'Half-acuted' is an inspired portmanteau (half + executed). The chicken comparison is unexpected and darkly funny — Ben's complete lack of moral weight on the subject of a botched execution is classic Veep nihilism.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Yes, but a statement that can be repurposed if things change. He dies, he doesn't die, or whatever.'
jokes.setup: Team debates whether to issue a statement on the botched execution
jokes.punchline: 'Or whatever' — treating a man's life or death as an afterthought in a political calculation
jokes.explanation: 'Or whatever' is the joke — the casual dismissal of life-or-death stakes as a political variable. Perfectly in character for Selina's moral vacuum. Economy is excellent; the 'or whatever' lands like a deflating balloon.

jokes.text: 'Dead kids... That never sounds good unless you're a stressed-out single mom.'
jokes.setup: Discovery that the opposition hacked data of dead children to target bereaved parents
jokes.punchline: Furlong's exception clause — 'unless you're a stressed-out single mom' — is outrageously dark and specific
jokes.explanation: Vintage Furlong — transgressive, specific, and delivered with complete confidence. The exception clause is what makes it brilliant: not just 'dead kids can be useful' but the pinpoint specificity of 'stressed-out single mom.' Dark but earns its shock through the precise absurdity.

jokes.text: 'There's only one midget I'm gonna let tell me what to do, and I'm gonna go tell her that I'm done.'
jokes.setup: Furlong is being told he can't resign; he's furious
jokes.punchline: Furlong refers to the President of the United States as 'the one midget I'll let tell me what to do' — his contempt expressed through a casually dehumanizing slur
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely about Furlong's character — his casual viciousness, his contempt for everyone, and his bizarre choice of insult. Character-integrated but leans somewhat on shock value.

jokes.text: 'What's she gonna do, pin a dick on Gary and give him the job?'
jokes.setup: Being told Selina doesn't have time to replace him
jokes.punchline: Furlong's imagining Gary (the put-upon bag man) as a fake-phallused campaign manager is absurd and cruel
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insults always escalate to absurdist places, and 'pin a dick on Gary' is a memorably strange image. The joke works because it targets Gary's emasculated status in the show while being maximally Furlong-brained.

jokes.text: 'Tough shitski'
jokes.setup: Being told there are a couple hours to replace him — supposedly enough time in politics
jokes.punchline: 'Tough shitski' — a Russified version of 'tough shit' that is entirely Furlong
jokes.explanation: Two words but highly memorable. The '-ski' suffix adds nothing logical but makes it funnier — it's Furlong's casual creativity with profanity. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: 'She's fucking ethnic! This is Latinageddon.'
jokes.setup: Learning that O'Brien's VP pick is Laura Montez — a brilliant, charming Latina woman
jokes.punchline: 'Latinageddon' — Selina's panic condensed into a coined portmanteau that exposes her political calculation over any actual racism
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Latinageddon' is a perfect neologism — funny, character-specific, and sharp political satire simultaneously. It captures Selina's moral vacuum (she's upset not because of any prejudice but because it threatens her), and the word itself is so clean and quotable that it functions as a standalone joke. Escalation from 'she's a woman!' to 'she's fucking ethnic!' to coining 'Latinageddon' in one breath is textbook Veep escalation.

jokes.text: 'I wish I was that guy on death row. Apparently he's vomiting black bile.'
jokes.setup: Montez's candidacy is a political catastrophe for Selina
jokes.punchline: She envies a half-executed man vomiting black bile over her own situation — then the detail about the black bile lands as a darkly absurd kicker
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback. The death row subplot pays off here perfectly — Selina's hyperbole is that she literally envies a dying man, and the black bile detail (which makes it worse) makes the callback a kicker rather than just a reference. Strong dark comedy with great economy.

jokes.text: 'He gets sexy Mexi. What am I stuck with? Doyle? Steve Martin's boring older brother.'
jokes.setup: Comparing O'Brien's exciting new VP pick to her own stodgy VP Doyle
jokes.punchline: 'Steve Martin's boring older brother' — a precision insult that makes Doyle's blandness vivid
jokes.explanation: Exceptional insult comedy. 'Sexy Mexi' is cringe-inducing but character-perfect. 'Steve Martin's boring older brother' is a masterpiece of the form — it uses Steve Martin's own whiteness and zanyness as the reference point, then imagines someone even blander. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'That would totally fuck up that fiesta of theirs, wouldn't it?'
jokes.setup: Selina suggests putting Danny Chung on the ticket to counter Montez
jokes.punchline: 'Fiesta' — reducing a Hispanic VP pick and its political implications to a party stereotype
jokes.explanation: Consistent with Selina's character but a weaker joke compared to 'Latinageddon' — the slur without the portmanteau invention. It's the expected Selina move at this point.

jokes.text: 'It's like Christmas except happy.'
jokes.setup: The team is elated that Doyle is leaving and Chung can take his place
jokes.punchline: Ben's deadpan caveat — Christmas apparently is not happy for him — buried inside a positive statement
jokes.explanation: A deceptively layered joke. On the surface it means 'I'm very happy.' But the embedded clause — 'except happy' — reveals Ben's bleak personal relationship with Christmas. Maximum information in minimum words. Pure Veep deadpan.

jokes.text: 'Now they can kill him properly. God, you'd think they could do that right. Death row... the clue is in the name.'
jokes.setup: They find a pulse on the death row inmate — meaning they can now proceed with execution
jokes.punchline: 'The clue is in the name' — Selina's exasperation at the state's incompetence at executing someone, treating it like a logistics failure
jokes.explanation: 'The clue is in the name' is a perfectly structured kicker for the running death-row gag. The logic — 'it's called death ROW, not almost-death row' — applies mundane consumer complaint logic to capital punishment. Strong running gag payoff.

jokes.text: Dan being sent to get coffee for the zucchini farmer and Jonah — he's become the coffee boy
jokes.setup: Sidney Purcell introduces himself as the power broker; Dan is apparently his subordinate
jokes.punchline: The man who bragged about his crammed Rolodex is immediately sent to fetch coffee, revealing his actual status
jokes.explanation: Structural reversal of Dan's earlier pomposity. The deflation is earned and well-timed.

jokes.text: The prostate cover story exchange: 'You have prostate problems.' / 'I'm not gonna say that.' / 'That's embarrassing.' / 'Exactly. That's what makes it more real.'
jokes.setup: They need Doyle to exit on health grounds to avoid a 'principle' narrative
jokes.punchline: Ben's logic: the more embarrassing the lie, the more credible it is — a perfectly cynical piece of political spin logic
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the political logic: embarrassment = credibility. Ben's tactical mind is perfectly rendered. The beat where Doyle says 'That's embarrassing' and Ben immediately says 'Exactly' is well-timed.

jokes.text: The coffee machine is claimed to be 'on the fritz' but Dan was told it worked fine earlier — Jonah obliviously orders a soy cappuccino anyway
jokes.setup: Dan is sent to get coffee as humiliation; he claims the machine is broken
jokes.punchline: Jonah and Richard not only don't register the humiliation but elaborate their coffee orders (soy cap, eggnog latte, biscotti) as if at a café — the scene escalates into absurd specificity
jokes.explanation: The comedy escalates beautifully: Dan's lie about the machine goes unacknowledged while Jonah and Richard pile on increasingly specific coffee orders. 'Eggnog latte if it's in season' from Richard is the peak absurdity. The obliviousness is character-perfect for both.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I'm the wheel-greaser. I'm the puppet master. A man of many nicknames. Poonslayer.' (phone buzzes) 'When I'm online. Video games?'
jokes.setup: Jonah is performing his importance as a Washington insider for the client
jokes.punchline: 'Poonslayer' immediately followed by the sheepish qualification 'when I'm online. Video games?' — his self-aggrandizement collapses in real time
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and one of the best Jonah jokes in the series. The escalation from 'wheel-greaser' to 'puppet master' to 'Poonslayer' — each more absurd — is perfectly structured. Then the instant, instinctive backpedal ('when I'm online. Video games?') deflates it completely. The specificity of 'video games?' as a question to himself is a perfect character beat. Highly quotable, deeply character-integrated, excellent structure.

jokes.text: Jonah describing himself: 'I'm the wheel-greaser. I'm the puppet master. A man of many nicknames. Poonslayer. When I'm online. Video games?'
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to impress a client by describing his political influence
jokes.punchline: The nicknames escalate from grandiose ('puppet master') to the immediately undermined 'Poonslayer' — an online gaming handle, not a political sobriquet — with the desperate parenthetical 'Video games?' trying to save face
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structural progression from 'wheel-greaser' to 'puppet master' builds a grandiose self-image, then 'Poonslayer. When I'm online. Video games?' systematically dismantles it in real time. The parenthetical clarifications are Jonah trying to unsay what he just said, which makes it funnier. Perfect Jonah character integration — this joke could only be him. High across all dimensions.

jokes.text: Dan claims Amy and he are 'closer than two fat guys in an elevator'; Richard immediately claims he 'used to date' Amy and knows her 'inside and out'; Jonah says he'll call her too because 'Ames' is actually 'long for Amy'
jokes.setup: Dan tries to leverage his Amy connection to save face with the client
jokes.punchline: Three men immediately one-up each other's Amy connections, each claim more ridiculous than the last, culminating in Jonah realizing 'Ames' is not actually short for anything
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured escalation. 'Two fat guys in an elevator' is an inventive simile for closeness. Richard's 'inside and out' claim (he dated her) is a beat funnier. Then Jonah's realization mid-sentence that 'Ames' is long for, not short for, Amy is the kicker. Each beat earns its place.

jokes.text: 'What did I do in a past life to deserve Karen? / I think you must have given the go-ahead for Pearl Harbor.'
jokes.setup: Selina bemoans Karen's uselessness
jokes.punchline: Ben's instant, specific historical parallel — you greenlit Pearl Harbor
jokes.explanation: The joke structure is perfect: rhetorical question met with a shockingly specific historical answer. Pearl Harbor is exactly the right scale of past-life sin to deserve Karen. Ben's instant certainty makes it funnier. High quotability.

jokes.text: Selina laughing reflexively at Chung's story: 'I love it when you tell that story. It's so funny.' — clearly performing
jokes.setup: Chung tells his Chungmania story
jokes.punchline: Selina's hollow, politician-smile response ('I love it when you tell that story') — she has clearly never heard it before
jokes.explanation: Callback to Joke 2 — Selina coaching Catherine on performing emotion she doesn't feel, now doing the same herself. The contradiction is on-brand and lands as a quick character note.

jokes.text: 'And my Bible. Although she is my Bible, so that's two Bibles.'
jokes.setup: Chung says he needs to consult his wife before deciding
jokes.punchline: Deciding his wife IS his Bible, creating the mathematical impossibility of two Bibles
jokes.explanation: Clever self-undermining logic — Chung realizes mid-sentence his metaphor creates a paradox and commits to it anyway. The 'so that's two Bibles' kicker is funny because he accepts rather than corrects the error.

jokes.text: 'Well, you take as many of the 15 minutes available as you need to make this decision.'
jokes.setup: Chung says he needs time to decide; Selina graciously says 'of course'
jokes.punchline: She immediately undercuts her graciousness by specifying 15 minutes — the urgency obliterating the pretense of patience
jokes.explanation: The structure is perfect: false magnanimity ('of course') followed immediately by a hard limit ('15 minutes'). The specificity of '15 minutes' is funnier than any round number. Classic Selina.

jokes.text: 'Well, you take as many of the 15 minutes available as you need to make this decision.'
jokes.setup: Chung says he needs time to consult his wife and Bible
jokes.punchline: Selina generously offers 'as many of the 15 minutes available as you need' — pretending to give him time while telegraphing the extreme urgency
jokes.explanation: The phrasing is beautifully constructed — 'as many of the 15 minutes available' mirrors the structure of a generous offer while being transparently coercive. Character-perfect for Selina: controlling, fake-magnanimous, on a deadline.

jokes.text: 'Oh, that's nice. Fuck off, I'm busy.'
jokes.setup: Pierce says he 'just wanted to wish you guys good luck'
jokes.punchline: Selina thanks him sincerely then immediately dismisses him with profanity
jokes.explanation: The rhythm is perfect: warmth then dismissal in six words. Economy is near-flawless. 'Oh, that's nice. Fuck off, I'm busy.' is immediately quotable.

jokes.text: '30 years as a senator and your asshole's getting more screen time than your face.'
jokes.setup: Doyle's prostate problems are being used as the cover story for his resignation
jokes.punchline: Furlong's formulation — screen time comparison between face and asshole — is inventively cruel
jokes.explanation: Premium Furlong. The 'screen time' metaphor is unexpected and perfectly calibrated — it makes a medical indignity feel like a Hollywood snub. The specificity of '30 years as a senator' grounds it before the twist. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'I understand you've been twirling Jonah's balls like they're some kind of exotic stress reducer.'
jokes.setup: Furlong is told about Teddy's sexual assault of Jonah
jokes.punchline: The 'exotic stress reducer' comparison is Furlong's characteristic way of making horror funny through absurd imagery
jokes.explanation: 'Exotic stress reducer' is a memorably strange image — it suggests a zen rock garden or a fidget toy, applied to sexual assault. Furlong's complete removal of moral weight makes it work as comedy. Dark but craft-driven.

jokes.text: 'I wish someone would sexually assault my staff. And by staff I mean penis.'
jokes.setup: Furlong hears about Teddy fondling Jonah
jokes.punchline: Furlong's stated wish to be sexually assaulted, followed by clarifying 'by staff I mean penis' — the clarification making it worse
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent: 'staff' works as a professional euphemism, then Furlong clarifies it's literally his penis — which is both worse and funnier. The self-correction sells it. Peak Furlong transgression.

jokes.text: 'I mean, which of us hasn't gotten off in some guy's junk? Just for a laugh?'
jokes.setup: Furlong continuing to process Teddy's assault of Jonah
jokes.punchline: Furlong normalizes the assault as a universal experience — 'just for a laugh' being the most deranged possible framing
jokes.explanation: The 'just for a laugh' construction is the key — the casualness is the joke. Slightly less sharp than the previous two Furlong lines in this sequence.

jokes.text: 'I want you to use your dirty hands, clean out your desk. You're fired.' / Teddy: 'Everything I have done has been to serve you.' / 'And that goes double for fondling Jonah.'
jokes.setup: Teddy pleads his devotion to Furlong as justification for everything he's done
jokes.punchline: 'And that goes double for fondling Jonah' — Furlong applies the devotion framing to the assault, making the assault an act of service
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic structure: Teddy's earnest plea sets up a straight-man moment, then Furlong's kicker recontextualizes the assault as loyalty. The 'that goes double for' construction is textbook Veep — taking a sincere phrase and applying it to something horrifying. One of the episode's best constructed jokes.

jokes.text: Doyle watching the Teddy scandal unfold on TV: 'Have you been watching this? This is a fascinating show.'
jokes.setup: Doyle is aware his aide is being fired for sexually assaulting Jonah
jokes.punchline: He watches it like a detached TV viewer — as if it's someone else's scandal
jokes.explanation: A nice character beat — Doyle's dissociation from his own political collapse is funny and revealing. Light but earned.

jokes.text: Doyle's prostate diagnosis on live TV: 'which is not good. Sounds like you might die.'
jokes.setup: Doyle is watching his prostate problem being discussed on television as the cover story
jokes.punchline: Selina's cheerful summary: 'Sounds like you might die'
jokes.explanation: Quick and dark. Selina's breezy delivery of 'sounds like you might die' to the man whose fake medical condition is being broadcast is a sharp character note.

jokes.text: 'What, are you knocked up? What's the real reason?'
jokes.setup: Chung declines, citing 'family issues'
jokes.punchline: Selina's first guess at 'family issues' is that Chung is pregnant
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of asking a man if he's pregnant as a sincere guess works through Selina's desperation-fueled non-sequitur logic. Her immediate pivot to 'what's the real reason' shows she knows it's nonsense.

jokes.text: Selina mimes a gunshot: 'And you never know. Someday somebody might just...' (imitates gunshot)
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to convince Chung to take the VP role by pointing out that presidents get assassinated, so VPs become presidents
jokes.punchline: She acts out an assassination with a hand gun gesture — to convince someone to join her ticket — a breathtakingly inappropriate political pitch
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's best moments. Selina is so desperate she uses her own potential assassination as a selling point, and the physical gesture (imitating a gunshot) makes it viscerally funny. The character integration is perfect: this is Selina's transactional relationship with her own mortality, her presidency, and her complete lack of political tact. The moment lands on the horror of what she just did. Exceptional.

jokes.text: 'The shitty, two-faced, fat fucking turd said no.'
jokes.setup: Ben asks diplomatically what Chung's response was
jokes.punchline: Selina's elaboration — 'let me elaborate on that' — delivers a string of escalating insults for Chung after his refusal
jokes.explanation: The 'let me elaborate' framing makes the profanity funnier — she's offering clarity that makes it worse. The four-adjective pile-on ('shitty, two-faced, fat fucking turd') is well-constructed.

jokes.text: 'I'm supposed to walk out on stage and then keep walking till I fall off the other side?'
jokes.setup: Crisis: no VP to announce, convention happening now
jokes.punchline: The visual of Selina walking off the edge of the stage as a solution
jokes.explanation: A good visual image used as self-deprecating crisis humor. The specificity of 'the other side' is a nice touch.

jokes.text: 'I like Tom James and I hate everyone.'
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Tom James as a VP option
jokes.punchline: Ben's endorsement — 'I like Tom James and I hate everyone' — is the highest possible praise from the show's most nihilistic character
jokes.explanation: Perfect line. Economy is maximum — seven words say everything about Ben's character AND provide a genuine character endorsement. The logic is clean: Ben's misanthropy is established enough that his exception proves the rule about Tom James. Highly quotable, perfectly character-integrated. Near the top of the episode.

jokes.text: Gary suggests asking Doyle back — everyone stares. Gary then suggests Maddox. 'Everybody stop having ideas.'
jokes.setup: The team is desperately brainstorming VP candidates
jokes.punchline: Gary's two-for-two terrible suggestions prompt Selina's command that everyone stop having ideas
jokes.explanation: 'Everybody stop having ideas' is a clean Selina line that works because it targets Gary specifically while pretending to be universal. The reaction beat of her vetoing Doyle back before Gary even finishes is also funny.

jokes.text: Death row inmate update: 'He died.' / 'Oh, finally, right?' / 'Death row... the clue is in the name.'
jokes.setup: Running death row subplot finally resolves
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'Oh, finally, right?' treats the execution as a completion of a task; reinforced by the earlier 'clue is in the name' callback
jokes.explanation: Good running gag payoff. 'Oh, finally, right?' as the reaction to a man's death is perfectly nihilistic. The subplot is well-managed across the episode.

jokes.text: Maddox: 'I'm a fiercely ambitious man. And I know how to get things done. Not that you don't. Though people say you don't.'
jokes.setup: Maddox is pitching himself for VP
jokes.punchline: He instinctively insults Selina mid-pitch — 'not that you don't. Though people say you don't' — completely sabotaging his own ask
jokes.explanation: The self-correction that makes it worse is a great comedic structure. He catches himself being rude, adds a qualifier, then immediately makes it worse again. The incremental digging ('not that you don't... though people say you don't') is well-paced.

jokes.text: Selina getting the Ben phone call and ejecting Maddox from the room mid-meeting with 'I've got to... He just closed the door, so I'm gonna have to head out'
jokes.setup: Maddox is mid-pitch; Ben calls
jokes.punchline: Selina's explanation — 'he just closed the door, so I'm gonna have to head out' — is nonsensical as a reason to leave and she knows it
jokes.explanation: The scrambled logic of 'he closed the door so I have to go' is a nice beat of Selina grabbing for any excuse. Quick and functional.

jokes.text: 'Well, that was then, and this is not then.'
jokes.setup: Ben points out Selina didn't like any of the pre-vetted VP options before
jokes.punchline: Selina's defense is a tautology: things were different then because it's not then anymore
jokes.explanation: Beautiful economy — ten words that reveal Selina's total disregard for consistency when it's inconvenient. 'This is not then' is the kind of line that sounds like it means something but is purely circular. Quotable as a deflection.

jokes.text: 'He's got a drinking problem. Yeah, shoe polish and he never even shared.'
jokes.setup: Olsen is raised as a VP option; 'he's got a drinking problem'
jokes.punchline: The detail that he drinks shoe polish AND didn't share it — the specificity of the substance and the pettiness of the grievance are both funny
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy. 'Shoe polish' escalates the drinking problem to an unexpected severity. The kicker — 'and he never even shared' — is the real joke, treating shoe polish like a social lubricant that should be offered around. Sharply original.

jokes.text: 'Are you trying to blow up the universe now? Who's gonna go for two women on the ticket? I guess we could all eat pussy all day long, too, right?'
jokes.setup: Staff suggests an all-female ticket (Leanne Harris)
jokes.punchline: Selina's dismissal spirals into a bizarrely specific crude non-sequitur — the logical leap from 'two women' to 'eat pussy' represents her panicked, unfiltered thinking
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina: her profanity-brain fires faster than her political-brain. The escalation from 'two women' to that specific non-sequitur exposes her panic and her lack of filter. Shock value is present but character-driven.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, that's why we 86'd him, Mike. The candidate needs to live up to and through the election.'
jokes.setup: 'What about Culverson?' / 'He's 86.'
jokes.punchline: 'That's why we 86'd him' — the slang '86'd' and the age '86' collide perfectly, with the added kicker that he needs to 'live up to and through the election'
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay. The double meaning of '86' (age / slang for eliminated) is exploited perfectly, and the follow-up — 'needs to live up to AND through' — adds a dark kicker about survival. Clean, well-structured, memorable.

jokes.text: Tom James working the convention floor: 'Hey, Natty. What a cool guy. You shit ice cubes and you piss snowflakes, right?'
jokes.setup: Tom James is greeting delegates with effortless charisma
jokes.punchline: His compliment to 'Natty' uses body functions — shitting ice cubes and pissing snowflakes — as a measure of someone's coolness
jokes.explanation: Highly original. The compliment-via-excretion logic is unexpected and vivid — 'you shit ice cubes' as the highest praise is a brilliant invention. It works because it characterizes Tom James as someone with irreverent charm. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Amy's resignation speech: 'I have bitten my tongue so long it looks like a dog's cushion.'
jokes.setup: Amy is finally exploding at Selina after weeks of suppressed frustration
jokes.punchline: The extended tongue metaphor — a dog's cushion — is an unexpected and vivid image for prolonged self-suppression
jokes.explanation: Brilliant image — 'dog's cushion' is unexpected and specific enough to be highly original. The metaphor earns its place in the middle of a dramatic speech, which makes it funnier. Amy's controlled rage finding this outlet is perfect character work.

jokes.text: 'You have two settings... No decision and bad decision.'
jokes.setup: Amy cataloguing Selina's failures in her resignation speech
jokes.punchline: The two-setting binary perfectly encapsulates Selina's decision-making throughout the episode
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'Two settings: no decision and bad decision' is the kind of formulation that works as pure comedy and as devastating character critique simultaneously. The binary-settings metaphor is clean and memorable. One of the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: 'I wouldn't let you run a bath without having the Coast Guard and the fire department standing by, but yet here you are running America.'
jokes.setup: Amy escalating her critique of Selina's competence
jokes.punchline: The progression from 'running a bath' to 'running America' — with emergency services required for the bath — is a perfect escalation
jokes.explanation: Superb construction. 'Run a bath / run America' is a perfect parallel, and inserting 'Coast Guard and fire department standing by' between the two makes the gap between bath and America comic. The joke works structurally and as character — Amy has been watching Selina fumble all episode.

jokes.text: 'You are the worst thing that has happened to this country since food in buckets and maybe slavery.'
jokes.setup: Amy's resignation speech climax
jokes.punchline: 'Food in buckets and maybe slavery' — the list comparison places Selina between a fast food convenience and chattel slavery, the 'maybe' hedging making it funnier
jokes.explanation: One of the great Veep lines. The list — 'food in buckets and maybe slavery' — is perfectly calibrated absurdism. 'Food in buckets' as a historical catastrophe (fast food? bucket meals?) is already funny; placing it before 'maybe slavery' with the hedging 'maybe' is extraordinary. The qualifier 'maybe' on slavery is what elevates this — it makes Amy seem like she's being fair-minded about a monstrous comparison. Near the top of the episode.

jokes.text: 'The fact that you are a woman means we will have no more women presidents because we tried one and she fucking sucked.'
jokes.setup: Amy's final blow in her resignation speech
jokes.punchline: 'We tried one and she fucking sucked' — the logic applies consumer-product trial logic to gender representation in politics
jokes.explanation: Devastating and brilliantly written. The logic — one failed trial ends the experiment for all women — is the most savage political satire in the episode. 'We tried one and she fucking sucked' applied to gender representation is exactly the kind of mordant joke Veep excels at. Highly quotable and the emotional peak of the resignation speech.

jokes.text: 'Well, that was clearly about me.' (laughs) / 'She's just one of those women who resents powerful women.'
jokes.setup: Amy has just delivered a devastating, specific, personal resignation speech directly at Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's complete inability to internalize any of it — reframing as generic jealousy — is the joke; plus her laughing as if it wasn't real
jokes.explanation: Perfect character punchline for the resignation speech. 'She's just one of those women who resents powerful women' applied to someone who explicitly said she's incompetent (not powerful) is a brilliant demonstration of Selina's armor. Also deeply ironic given that Amy's speech was specifically about Selina failing women. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'She's just one of those women who resents powerful women.'
jokes.setup: Selina processes Amy's departure
jokes.punchline: She immediately recasts Amy — who has dedicated years of her career to Selina — as an envious woman who resents powerful women
jokes.explanation: The immediate defensiveness, the ironic recasting (Amy was arguably the competent one), and the familiar political trope being deployed in the most self-serving possible way makes this one of Selina's great character-defining moments. The economy is exceptional — it tells you exactly how Selina processes criticism.

jokes.text: 'What do you think of Tom James?' / Karen: 'What do I think of him? I think there's a lot to think.'
jokes.setup: One final check with Karen after everyone else has moved on
jokes.punchline: Karen's response is literally just the question restated as a noun phrase — 'there is a lot to think' about thinking
jokes.explanation: The Karen character arc gets one last perfect beat before her firing. 'There's a lot to think' is the purest expression of her pathology — she has converted a question into a statement about the existence of thoughts without having any. Peak Karen.

jokes.text: Selina's 'Ank you' to Karen — a thanks so abbreviated it's basically contempt
jokes.setup: Karen gives her non-answer
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Ank you' — so contemptuous she can't even finish 'Thank'
jokes.explanation: A tiny moment but very well-played — the dropped 'Th' signals complete dismissal. Economy is maximum.

jokes.text: Dan discovers Amy has quit via mass text — 'Holy shit, Amy's gone' / 'Holy shit, that's insane. Wow, I'm getting the same thing, guys. Maybe we're getting the same text message. Oh, yeah, we are.'
jokes.setup: Amy's resignation goes public via group text
jokes.punchline: Jonah and Richard process the information in real time, aloud, realizing they received the same text — the obvious being announced as a discovery
jokes.explanation: Jonah and Richard's simultaneous obvious realization ('maybe we got the same text — oh yeah, we did') is a gentle joke about their shared dimness. Works as a character beat but isn't especially inventive.

jokes.text: 'All right, bottom line, we cannot let Mr. Zucchini the human vegetable find out about this.'
jokes.setup: Dan trying to manage the situation as Amy's departure becomes public
jokes.punchline: 'Mr. Zucchini the human vegetable' — a casual nickname for Ian that doubles as an insult
jokes.explanation: 'Human vegetable' as descriptor for the zucchini magnate is a clean insult that connects back to the subplot. Quick and functional.

jokes.text: 'I have a little black book, okay? I have numbers the NSA doesn't even know about. Have you heard of Mike McClintock?'
jokes.setup: Ian is threatening to leave; Dan tries to retain him by name-dropping contacts
jokes.punchline: His secret contact — numbers the NSA doesn't know — is Mike McClintock, the show's least impressive press secretary
jokes.explanation: The escalating pomposity ('numbers the NSA doesn't know') collapsing into 'Mike McClintock' is a perfect structural joke. Works best for viewers who know Mike's reputation.

jokes.text: 'Best one in four years.' — Tom James on the convention
jokes.setup: Tom James says 'what a great convention'
jokes.punchline: 'Best one in four years' — conventions are every four years, so this is definitionally the only one in the period
jokes.explanation: Smart political joke — 'best in four years' sounds like a compliment but is a tautology since there's only one convention per cycle. Tom James's smooth delivery makes you hear the joke a beat late.

jokes.text: 'He has one skeleton. You're looking at her.' — Ben about why he didn't want to suggest Tom James
jokes.setup: Someone says 'everyone has skeletons' while arguing for Tom James; Ben has been holding back
jokes.punchline: Ben reveals his objection: the 'skeleton' is his ex — who is Amy, now the fired campaign manager who just had a meltdown
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure — 'you're looking at her' pointing at Amy is the reveal that connects two plots. Economy is great, and the callback implication (Tom dated Amy, Amy just quit in a meltdown) makes this land harder on rewatch.

jokes.text: Tom James arrives at the door: 'Room service.' — Gary nearly has a heart attack
jokes.setup: Team is waiting anxiously for Tom James; Gary says 'Oh, God!' when someone knocks
jokes.punchline: Tom James's breezy 'Room service' opener deflates the tension instantly — he's already performing charming ease
jokes.explanation: Two words that do a lot — 'Room service' establishes Tom James's effortless charm immediately and Gary's shocked 'Oh, God!' is a perfect reaction beat. The visual of Gary's relief/shock completes the joke.

jokes.text: 'I could murder and make a lampshade out of a Manhattan.' — Tom James ordering a drink
jokes.setup: Tom James is offered something to drink
jokes.punchline: His enthusiasm for a Manhattan expressed through violence and craft — 'murder and make a lampshade' — is both charming and disturbing
jokes.explanation: 'Murder and make a lampshade out of' is a genuinely original way to express desire for a drink — the 'lampshade' addition (as in, someone who acts silly at parties) adds a layer. Highly quotable and perfectly character-integrated for Tom James's breezy, slightly dangerous charm.

jokes.text: Tom: 'I could murder and make a lampshade out of a Manhattan.' (All laugh) Gary immediately: 'I would like something to drink. Bourbon, neat.'
jokes.setup: Tom James is offered a drink; he makes a colorful joke about wanting a Manhattan
jokes.punchline: His 'murder and make a lampshade out of' as a creative way to say 'I really want one' gets a laugh from the room; then Gary immediately, seriously, orders a bourbon neat — unable to match Tom's wit but making the same decision
jokes.explanation: Tom's colorful phrasing ('murder and make a lampshade out of a Manhattan') is inventive and immediately gets a room laugh. Gary's straight 'Bourbon, neat' after is the perfect straight-man follow — he wants alcohol too, none of the charm. The juxtaposition is the joke.

jokes.text: Selina dismisses Gary to get bourbon, then tells him to go straighten her room — two separate menial tasks in quick succession while Tom James watches
jokes.setup: Tom James arrives for an important meeting; Gary is hovering
jokes.punchline: Selina treats Gary like household staff in front of a potential VP, making Gary fetch bourbon then clean the room while she navigates a crucial political moment
jokes.explanation: Standard Gary humiliation beat — functional but not particularly inventive. The joke is the contrast of context (huge meeting) vs. task (clean my room), which is a recurring show dynamic rather than a specific joke.

jokes.text: Tom James's fake-out acceptance: 'I'm afraid, with deep regret... That I'm going to have to accept.'
jokes.setup: Selina offers Tom the VP spot; Tom begins with 'I'm afraid, with deep regret...'
jokes.punchline: 'That I'm going to have to accept' — the regretful framing is applied to acceptance, inverting the convention of formal declination
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structured jokes. The misdirection is perfectly set up by Chung's earlier actual rejection (which followed the same formal cadence). Tom applies the language of reluctant refusal to enthusiastic acceptance. The structure is clean, the timing (after Chung's real rejection) earns maximum impact, and 'with deep regret' is instantly quotable.

jokes.text: Tom James retelling his own joke to Mike — 'Again?' / 'Yeah, definitely tell him. Please. Can't wait. It's hilarious.'
jokes.setup: Selina and Tom want to share the joke with Mike
jokes.punchline: Tom performs the retelling enthusiastically again while Selina says 'you didn't say that. He did not say that' — adding another layer of disbelief
jokes.explanation: The retelling works as a comedic device — it's a callback that's self-aware, and the team's giddiness ('can't wait') is charming. Slightly less impact on the third iteration.

jokes.text: 'This is the best announcement since all those pandas got pregnant.'
jokes.setup: Tom James has accepted the VP offer; everyone is celebrating
jokes.punchline: Mike's bizarre, heartfelt comparison to a mass panda pregnancy — as if this is a cultural touchstone everyone shares
jokes.explanation: Exceptional absurdist joke. 'All those pandas got pregnant' is specific, unexpected, and somehow deeply Mike — his PR brain makes him think in terms of public mood events, and apparently a mass panda pregnancy was the previous high. The reply 'I don't know what that means' from Tom James is the perfect deadpan landing. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Tom James: 'Oh, it is with deep regret I must work.' — reprising his own joke immediately
jokes.setup: Selina tells Tom he needs to go get things going
jokes.punchline: Tom applies 'with deep regret' to leaving the celebration — the running gag now has a third iteration with a new application
jokes.explanation: Third beat of the running gag — works because it's Tom himself deploying it casually now, suggesting it's already become his signature. The gag has earned this third iteration.

jokes.text: Tom James reveals he wants to sit down with Amy Brookheimer — Selina covers: 'Kent. Roger that.' — Kent's immediate, robotic 'Roger that' to his new role
jokes.setup: Tom asks about Amy; Selina says she 'had to let her go' and that Kent is now campaign manager
jokes.punchline: Kent's instant 'Roger that' to being spontaneously appointed as campaign manager — with no reaction — is perfectly robotic
jokes.explanation: Kent's two-word response to being appointed campaign manager with no consultation is perfect character comedy. 'Roger that' as campaign acceptance is both militaristically appropriate and deeply funny for Kent's character.

jokes.text: Kent firing Karen: 'Your champagne's been laced with polonium. I mean, not really. It's just to lighten up the moment a little bit. But she definitely wants you to go.'
jokes.setup: Kent must fire Karen (diplomatically)
jokes.punchline: Kent's idea of lightening a moment is joking about poison — then immediately clarifying — then immediately confirming she's fired
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. Every beat works: Kent attempting to 'lighten the moment' with a polonium joke, the earnest clarification ('not really'), then the explanation that it was meant to be light ('just to lighten up the moment a little bit'), then the immediate delivery of the bad news anyway. It's perfectly character-integrated — Kent's idea of being warm is technically calibrated and fails completely. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Karen's response to being fired: 'I don't know what to think.' / Kent: 'Precisely. You can just use that... the door right there.'
jokes.setup: Karen is fired; she says she doesn't know what to think
jokes.punchline: Kent's 'precisely' — her inability to form thoughts is finally being used as a reason to dismiss her — with a gentle redirect to the door
jokes.explanation: The Karen arc pays off perfectly here. Her defining flaw (never knowing what to think or say) is the mechanism of her own dismissal. 'Precisely' as the agreement with 'I don't know what to think' is the most efficient possible punchline — it weaponizes her own indecision against her. Beautifully structured.

jokes.text: Convention stage: Selina and Tom bump teeth on their big kiss. Selina tells the crowd: 'It's funny because we actually bumped teeth there... we have our whole lives to practice that.'
jokes.setup: Selina and Tom James do the victory kiss on stage for the convention crowd
jokes.punchline: They visibly botch it (bumped teeth) and Selina, ever the politician, covers it with a joke — 'we have our whole lives to practice' — which is both awkward and foreshadowing
jokes.explanation: A callback to the rehearsal kiss being terrible — even the real thing fails. Selina's recovery is in character (she spins everything). 'We have our whole lives to practice' has rewatch value knowing what happens to the Selina/Tom dynamic.

jokes.text: 'You leave this man alone, okay? Precious cargo right here.' / 'Well, he's my cheerleader.' / 'Yeah, I've got to get him pom-poms.'
jokes.setup: Selina and Tom interacting warmly on stage — Selina calls Gary 'precious cargo'
jokes.punchline: Tom's 'he's my cheerleader' / Selina's 'I've got to get him pom-poms' — casually reducing Gary to a decorative accessory in front of the crowd
jokes.explanation: Light Gary humiliation beat. 'Pom-poms' is a slightly funnier image than usual for the Gary-as-object jokes but doesn't reach the craft of better moments.

jokes.text: Tom: 'Do you ever get tired of this?' / Selina: 'Really tired of it, yeah.'
jokes.setup: On stage, receiving thunderous applause from a convention crowd
jokes.punchline: Selina's genuine, exhausted 'Really tired of it, yeah' — in the middle of the triumphant moment she's been scrambling for all episode
jokes.explanation: A perfect closing note. The irony of Selina expressing exhaustion at the exact moment of her greatest triumph is deeply character-consistent — she has wanted this her whole life and it has ground her down. 'Really tired of it, yeah' said while smiling through applause is a micro-tragedy rendered as comedy. The economy is exceptional: four words closing an entire episode arc.

jokes.text: Final line: Tom: 'Do you ever get tired of this?' / Selina: 'Really tired of it, yeah.'
jokes.setup: The convention crowd is cheering; Tom asks if Selina gets tired of the adulation
jokes.punchline: 'Really tired of it, yeah' — Selina's honest exhaustion breaking through the performance at the exact moment of her greatest triumph
jokes.explanation: A beautiful closing joke. The contrast between the crowd's ecstasy and Selina's honest 'really tired of it' is perfectly understated. The character integration is excellent — Selina's exhaustion and ambivalence about the performance of politics is the show's central theme. 'Yeah' at the end makes it land softer, which is right for a closing note.

tmdb_overview: Selina and her staff scramble to avert a major internal crisis; Amy is angry with a new advisor.
insight: Convention chaos reaches 81.2 with 64 jokes—Selina's damage control spirals fastest here.
